teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the idea that industrial robots are powerful, predictable machines only when the human system around them is disciplined.
teaching_arc.progression: Move from hazard recognition to safeguards, procedures, and concrete lab behavior.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should be able to explain what makes a robot cell safe and what unsafe behavior looks like.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Make students treat robot safety as an operating discipline rather than a checklist they memorize once.
topic_takeaways: Students should recognize the main sources of industrial robot risk.
Students should understand how safeguarding, procedures, and behavior work together.
Students should leave with a few non-negotiable habits they can apply in lab immediately.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Use firm language when explaining consequences.
Keep props minimal so the instructor presence stays central.
transition_style: Escalate from what can go wrong to what prevents it, then reinforce the operating rules students must carry into lab.

slides.narration_seed: Welcome everyone. Before we touch any robot, we’re going to set one standard for this lab: safety comes first, every time.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Make students treat robot safety as an operating discipline rather than a checklist they memorize once.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Safety: Why is it so important? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Robot Safety for Us as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Robot Safety for Us connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Robot Safety for Us sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slide_ref.source_title: Safety: Why is it so important?

slides.narration_seed: Industrial robots are fantastic tools; fast, precise, and incredibly useful for automation. But that same power makes them dangerous if we treat them casually.
must_cover: Industrial Robots are powerful tools
Extremely useful in automation
Extremely dangerous if improperly handled
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
Impacts between humans and robots can break bones
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Robot Safety for Us into Safety: Why is it so important? so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Safety: Why is it so important? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Explain Safety: Why is it so important? like an operating warning: state the risk, the consequence, and the safe rule.
slides.story_hint: Use a believable lab or shop-floor mistake rather than abstract warning language.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Safety: Why is it so important? wrong and remember the safe rule.

slide_ref.source_title: Safety: Why is it so important?

slides.narration_seed: This point is critical: most robot accidents do not happen during normal production runs. They happen when people are close to the robot; programming, touch-up, maintenance, repair, testing, setup, or adjustments.
must_cover: … many robot accidents do not occur under normal operating conditions but, instead during programming, program touch-up or refinement, maintenance, repair, testing, setup, or adjustment.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Safety: Why is it so important? into Safety: Why is it so important? so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.

slides.story_hint: Use a believable lab or shop-floor mistake rather than abstract warning language.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Safety: Why is it so important? wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Let’s use this question to wake up our ‘safety brain.’ The correct answer is B. I want you to connect that to our lab reality: we will be doing programming, touch-up, and setup frequently; meaning we have to be more disciplined than a typical production operator.
must_cover: When do many robot accidents happen (according to the slide)?
Most accidents do not occur under normal operation, but during programming/touch-up/maintenance/testing/setup/adjustment.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Quick question (class discussion) wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Now; Emergency Stops. Before you run anything, you must physically identify the E-stops in your area.
must_cover: Before working with any robot, you must know the location of the emergency stops.
If an emergency occurs, the robot behaves unexpectedly, the robot impacts an obstacle, or anything else dangerous, press the button.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Safety: Safe Behaviour around Robots so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Explain Safety: Emergency Stops like an operating warning: state the risk, the consequence, and the safe rule.
slides.story_hint: Use a believable lab or shop-floor mistake rather than abstract warning language.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Safety: Emergency Stops wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: These are the behaviors that keep you safe. First: a visual inspection before startup; look for anything that could cause a collision, a snag, or a surprise.
must_cover: Always give the robot or automated system a visual inspection before startup for any potential for collisions or other safety hazards.
Keep the entire robot or automated system area free of clutter and debris.
Remove any dangly jewelry before working on the robot.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
Do not wear loose or flowing clothing while working on the robot.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Safety: Safe Behaviour around Robots wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Quick pair activity. You have about one minute.
must_cover: Where is the working radius (work envelope) for our robot cell?
What would count as “clutter and debris” around our setup today?
Name two things you should remove or avoid wearing before working on the robot.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.narration_seed: This is our operating rule in this lab: always work in groups. One person operates, one person is ready on the E-stop, and others observe and communicate.
must_cover: ALWAYS WORK IN GROUPS
Always have one person on the emergency stop
The emergency stop will stop the robot from moving when pressed.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
Always double check connections
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Quick check (pair activity) into Safety: Safe Behaviour around Robots so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Safety: Safe Behaviour around Robots wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Correct answer is B. In a real workplace, roles exist for a reason.
must_cover: During programming/touch-up work, what is a safe team setup?
The slide states: ALWAYS WORK IN GROUPS and always have one person on the emergency stop.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.narration_seed: Preventative maintenance is mostly good observation. Before you start, check the robot is stable and properly bolted down.
must_cover: Make sure the robot is properly bolted down and that the bolts have not loosened
Make sure that there are no obstacles in the robot’s working radius
Make sure that all cables are properly connected, that none are loose or damaged
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
On power up, ensure that the controller turns on
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.narration_seed: Here’s an example of an engineered safety control: infrared light curtains. There’s a transmitter and a receiver creating a ‘wall’ of invisible beams.
must_cover: State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.narration_seed: This is what a guarded workcell looks like. Barriers, fencing, and controlled access keep people out of the robot’s danger zone during operation.
must_cover: State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.narration_seed: Signs and taped regions are simple, but effective. Signs remind you of hazards and change behavior; especially for visitors or people unfamiliar with the cell.
must_cover: Signs: Signs change the behaviour of users to avoid dangerous behaviours. The signs should be flashy and eye catching
Taped Off Regions: The area the robot can reach should be taped off to inform those around it that the area should not be entered when the robot is on.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.narration_seed: Let’s think like technicians. If the boundary is unclear, you don’t run.
must_cover: What should the group do before running the robot?
Who should you inform?
How do you prevent someone else from stepping into the working radius?
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.narration_seed: We need to be realistic: collisions can happen. If it happens, the first action is always E-stop; stop motion.
must_cover: Unfortunately there may come a time when the robot collides with one of us in this class

If this happens, press the E-Stop and help release the person if they are being pinched or pinned. (This may involve limping the robot arm, or releasing the robot brakes. Take your time to figure this out before moving the person.)
Tell your Professor immediately – if you are dealing with the casualty and the Professor is not right at hand, send someone else for the professor.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
If there is an obvious injury, get someone to dial ‘5000’ on the Emergency Phone at the front of the lab. Tell them you need First Aid, and give them the lab number – T221
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Human Impact wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: This is the emergency sequence I want you to memorize. Press the E-stop.
must_cover: Press the E-stop.
Keep calm.
Send someone to get help.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
If someone is pinned, move the robot by hand to release them.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slide_ref.source_title: Crossover: Electrical safety in a robot lab

slides.narration_seed: Quick crossover reminder: robot labs are not only mechanical hazards. We also work around electricity; controllers, power supplies, cabinets, wiring.
must_cover: It’s the current through your body that causes the injury; voltage drives current and time matters.
Shock severity depends on conditions (skin condition, wetness, contact area, and the path through the body).
Arc flash is a major danger: extreme heat, pressure wave, blinding light, flying debris/projectiles, and fumes.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Human Impact into Crossover: Electrical safety in a robot lab so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Crossover: Safe work approach (lab mindset) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Explain Crossover: Electrical safety in a robot lab like an operating warning: state the risk, the consequence, and the safe rule.
slides.story_hint: Use a believable lab or shop-floor mistake rather than abstract warning language.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Crossover: Electrical safety in a robot lab wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Here’s the lab mindset I expect: assume live until you prove it’s dead. We avoid live work unless there’s a justified reason and the proper training, planning, and PPE are in place.
must_cover: Assume a circuit is live until proven otherwise.
Avoid working live unless trained and required ; if it must be done, plan hazards, PPE, and steps first.
Use a safe verification approach to confirm “dead” before touching (meter check → target check → meter check again).
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
Use lockout/tagout principles when equipment is being serviced or left for another person/shift.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Crossover: Safe work approach (lab mindset) wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Correct answer is B. ‘Robot not moving’ does not mean ‘safe to touch wiring.’ The safe default is: assume live until proven otherwise.
must_cover: You need to touch wiring inside a control cabinet. What is the safest first mindset before you put your hands in?
Good lab mindset: assume live until proven otherwise, then verify safely.
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
